local_names.thai: ข้าวกล้องหุงสุก
local_names.romanized_local: Cāomǐ Fàn (Chinese); Nasi Perang (Malay); Khao Klong Hoong Suk (Thai)

aliases: ข้าวกล้องหุงสุก

thai: ข้าวกล้องหุงสุก

region_of_origin: Southeast Asia / China (Oryza sativa); widely consumed across Singapore, Thailand, Malaysia
food_subgroup: Whole grain rice — unmilled or minimally milled rice retaining the bran layer and germ; long-grain or jasmine brown rice variety

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked brown rice as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for specific cooked grain preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Singapore serving of cooked brown rice is approximately 180g (one rice bowl), providing around 221 kcal, 4.7g protein, 46.1g carbohydrate, and 2.9g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Brown Rice Cooked — USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878 reports cooked long-grain brown rice per 100g. Values used directly without adaptation. This is the canonical reference for cooked brown rice nutrient composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database: brown rice long-grain cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 50 for long-grain cooked brown rice sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (range 42–58 across studies). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 44.3g for 180g serving) / 100 = 22.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1mg/100g); meaningful magnesium (43mg/100g) and potassium (79mg/100g) content
hypertension.guidance: Cooked brown rice prepared without added salt contains negligible sodium (~1mg/100g) and provides magnesium and potassium that support healthy blood pressure; it is an appropriate staple grain for individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~50 (low), GL ~12.3 per 100g — significantly lower glycaemic response than white rice (GI ~72) but still a meaningful carbohydrate load at typical Singapore serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brown rice has a GI of approximately 50 versus white rice GI ~72, representing a 28% lower glycaemic response, making it a preferred rice choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control remains essential as a 150–200g serving still delivers 38–51g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre relative to white rice, and presence of gamma-oryzanol and tocotrienols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cooked brown rice is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and its bran layer provides gamma-oryzanol and tocotrienols with antioxidant and LDL-lowering properties, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High magnesium (43mg/100g) and antioxidant phytochemicals (gamma-oryzanol, tocotrienols) in bran layer
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brown rice provides approximately 43mg magnesium per 100g — significantly more than white rice (~12mg) — which is relevant for rheumatoid arthritis management as magnesium deficiency is associated with elevated inflammatory markers CRP and IL-6.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Rice, Cooked (per 100g) is long-grain or jasmine brown rice (Oryza sativa) cooked in water, retaining its outer bran layer and germ. It provides approximately 123 kcal, 2.6g protein, 1.0g fat (0.2g saturated), 25.6g carbohydrate, 1.6g dietary fibre, and only 1mg sodium per 100g. The texture is slightly chewier and nuttier than white rice due to the intact bran layer. Glycemic index is approximately 50 (low GI, University of Sydney GI Database), compared to white rice GI ~72 — a 28% lower glycaemic response — making it the preferred rice choice for diabetes dietary management in Singapore. Brown rice contains 4.5× more dietary fibre than white rice and is rich in B vitamins (thiamine 0.09mg, niacin 1.5mg, B6 0.15mg), magnesium (43mg), phosphorus (83mg), and selenium (9.8mcg) concentrated in the bran. The bran also provides gamma-oryzanol and tocotrienols with antioxidant and cardioprotective properties. Widely sold at Singapore NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage as Thai brown rice or organic long-grain brown rice. Standard dietary prescription for Type 2 Diabetes management in Singapore. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: brown rice vs white rice nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878 (exact match for cooked long-grain brown rice). GI value of 50 confirmed by University of Sydney GI Database for long-grain cooked brown rice. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved primary source. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC 168878; brown rice contains negligible free sugars as the carbohydrate is predominantly starch. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain food. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0, vitamin_c_mg is 0.0, vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0, and vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC 168878; brown rice does not naturally contain these nutrients. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC 168878. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.37g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.36g) sourced from USDA FDC 168878 fatty acid breakdown. Water_g (70.3g) sourced from USDA FDC 168878. GL per serving calculated at 180g serving size (typical Singapore rice bowl): (50 × 44.3g carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.1. GI source is University of Sydney GI Database; range 42–58 across studies, central estimate 50 used. No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked brown rice as a specific food product. FoodOn URI FOODON_03306089 and FoodEx2 code A0GRC are carried forward from input record as defensible ontology anchors.
